{"author":{"id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MTQxNjcy","is_bot":false,"login":"mseele","name":"Michael Seele"},"body":"## What to build

Convert `src/logic/events.rs` (2,351 LOC, 18 public functions) into a directory module. The first vertical slice extracts **only** the notification fan-out: the ~4 functions that build notification emails and the templates they use.

Target shape:

```
src/logic/events/
  mod.rs           # pub use siblings; the remaining ~14 fns
  notifications.rs # the 4 notification fns + their templates
```

Rules for this slice:
- Only notification functions move. The booking lifecycle, payment operations, and event-query functions stay where they are. Do **not** cascade this slice into further splits — those are follow-up issues.
- `events::notifications` takes `&impl EmailGateway` directly (the seam from #71) and stops being a sibling of the rest of `events.rs` for the purpose of email access.
- `api.rs` call sites for the moved functions update to `events::notifications::*`.
- Notification tests move next to `notifications.rs` and run offline via the `EmailGateway` mock from #71.

This is the first of several vertical slices on the events module — this one carries the proof that the new seams work in the biggest module in the codebase.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `src/logic/events/` directory with `mod.rs` + `notifications.rs` (+ any remaining siblings not touched by this slice)
- [ ] Exactly the 4 notification functions move; no other code moves
- [ ] `events::notifications` accepts `&impl EmailGateway` and no longer reaches across to the rest of `events.rs` for email access
- [ ] Notification tests live next to `notifications.rs` and run fully offline via the `EmailGateway` mock
- [ ] No behavior change in production paths — same emails go to the same recipients with the same content
- [ ] `cargo test` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy --all-features` is clean

## Follow-ups (out of scope for this slice)

- Extract `events::payments` into its own module
- Extract `events::bookings` into its own module
- Extract `events::queries` into its own module

Each of those is its own vertical slice and deserves its own issue after this one lands.

## Blocked by

- #71 (Collapse the email seam into a deep EmailGateway) — the new module consumes `EmailGateway` directly, so the seam must exist first.","number":77,"state":"OPEN","title":"Extract events::notifications into its own module to improve notification tests","url":"https://github.com/mseele/sve-backend/issues/77"}

state.output:
[open] Issue #71: Collapse the email seam into a deep EmailGateway to improve tests and remove mock sprawl
  Author: @mseele
  Status: OPEN
  URL: https://github.com/mseele/sve-backend/issues/71

  Description:
    ## What to build
    
    Reshape the existing `EmailSender` trait in `src/email.rs` (5 methods, each a 1-line wrapper around a free function) into a deep `EmailGateway` trait with three methods:
    
    - `send_messages(from: &EmailAccount, messages: Vec<Message>) -> Result<()>`
    - `build_message(account: &EmailAccount, ...) -> Result<Message>` (move `new_message_builder` and `mailbox` inside the module)
    - `account_by_type(email_type: EmailType) -> Result<EmailAccount>`
    
    Delete the three redundant mock constructors in `src/test_utils.rs` (`noop_mock`, `mock_email_sender_capturing`, `mock_email_sender_capturing_batch`); keep one canonical mock constructor. Logic modules stop calling `crate::email::new_message_builder` and `crate::email::mailbox` as free functions — those become private to the `EmailGateway` implementation.
    
    This is a vertical slice: the trait, the real adapter, the test adapter, the call-site migration, and the test that exercises the new seam.
    
    ## Acceptance criteria
    
    - [ ] `EmailGateway` trait has at most 3 methods
    - [ ] No logic module calls `email::new_message_builder` or `email::mailbox` directly (the leak across the seam is sealed)
    - [ ] `src/test_utils.rs` has exactly 1 mock constructor (down from 4)
    - [ ] `RealEmailGateway` (or renamed equivalent) replaces `RealEmailSender`
    - [ ] All existing email tests still pass
    - [ ] `cargo test` passes
    - [ ] `cargo clippy --all-features` is clean
    
    ## Blocked by
    
    None - can start immediately

# Issue tracker: GitHub

Issues and PRDs for this repo live as GitHub issues. Use the `gh` CLI for all operations.

## Conventions

- **Create an issue**: `gh issue create --title "..." --body "..."`. Use a heredoc for multi-line bodies.
- **Read an issue**: `gh issue view <number> --comments`, filtering comments by `jq` and also fetching labels.
- **List issues**: `gh issue list --state open --json number,title,body,labels,comments --jq '[.[] | {number, title, body, labels: [.labels[].name], comments: [.comments[].body]}]'` with appropriate `--label` and `--state` filters.
- **Comment on an issue**: `gh issue comment <number> --body "..."`
- **Apply / remove labels**: `gh issue edit <number> --add-label "..."` / `--remove-label "..."`
- **Close**: `gh issue close <number> --comment "..."`

Infer the repo from `git remote -v` — `gh` does this automatically when run inside a clone.

## Pull requests as a triage surface

**PRs as a request surface: no.** _(Set to `yes` if this repo treats external PRs as feature requests; `/triage` reads this flag.)_

When set to `yes`, PRs run through the same labels and states as issues, using the `gh pr` equivalents:

display.text:
# Issue tracker: GitHub

Issues and PRDs for this repo live as GitHub issues. Use the `gh` CLI for all operations.

## Conventions

- **Create an issue**: `gh issue create --title "..." --body "..."`. Use a heredoc for multi-line bodies.
- **Read an issue**: `gh issue view <number> --comments`, filtering comments by `jq` and also fetching labels.
- **List issues**: `gh issue list --state open --json number,title,body,labels,comments --jq '[.[] | {number, title, body, labels: [.labels[].name], comments: [.comments[].body]}]'` with appropriate `--label` and `--state` filters.
- **Comment on an issue**: `gh issue comment <number> --body "..."`
- **Apply / remove labels**: `gh issue edit <number> --add-label "..."` / `--remove-label "..."`
- **Close**: `gh issue close <number> --comment "..."`

Infer the repo from `git remote -v` — `gh` does this automatically when run inside a clone.

## Pull requests as a triage surface

**PRs as a request surface: no.** _(Set to `yes` if this repo treats external PRs as feature requests; `/triage` reads this flag.)_

When set to `yes`, PRs run through the same labels and states as issues, using the `gh pr` equivalents:

- **Read a PR**: `gh pr view <number> --comments` and `gh pr diff <number>` for the diff.
- **List external PRs for triage**: `gh pr list --state open --json number,title,body,labels,author,authorAssociation,comments` then keep only `authorAssociation` of `CONTRIBUTOR`, `FIRST_TIME_CONTRIBUTOR`, or `NONE` (drop `OWNER`/`MEMBER`/`COLLABORATOR`).
- **Comment / label / close**: `gh pr comment`, `gh pr edit --add-label`/`--remove-label`, `gh pr close`.

GitHub shares one number space across issues and PRs, so a bare `#42` may be either — resolve with `gh pr view 42` and fall back to `gh issue view 42`.

## When a skill says "publish to the issue tracker"

Create a GitHub issue.

## When a skill says "fetch the relevant ticket"

Run `gh issue view <number> --comments`.

17:     let mut mock = MockEmailSender::new();
18:

25:                 let account = account.clone();
26:                 Box::pin(async move { Ok(account) })
27:             });

31:         .returning(|_, _| Box::pin(async { Ok(()) }));
32:     mock.expect_send_messages()

35:     mock

380: pub(crate) async fn send_event_email(
381:     pool: &PgPool,
382:     data: EventEmail,
383:     email_sender: &impl email::EmailSender,
384: ) -> Result<()> {
385:     if !data.bookings && !data.waiting_list {
386:         bail!("Either bookings or waiting list option need to be selected to send an event email.")
387:     }

458: /// send a reminder email for each events that starts next week
459: pub(crate) async fn send_event_reminders(

462: ) -> Result<usize> {
463:     // get all events where a event reminder should be send to the subscribers
464:     let events = db::get_reminder_events(pool).await?;
465: 
466:     // process each event
467:     for event in &events {
468:         // prepare for message generation

477:                 include_str!("../../templates/event_reminder_fitness.txt"),

481:                 include_str!("../../templates/event_reminder_events.txt"),
482:             ),
483:         };
484: 
485:         // iterate all enrolled event subscribers (Option should never be None)
486:         if let Some(subscribers) = &event.subscribers {
487:             for subscriber in subscribers.iter().filter(|s| s.enrolled) {
488:                 // render the body for the email...
489:                 let body = template::render_event_reminder(body, event, subscriber)?;

510:         }
511:     }
512:

OK so the 4 notification functions are:
1. `send_event_email` (380-456)
2. `send_event_reminders` (459-515)
3. `send_payment_reminders` (518-584)
4. `send_participation_confirmation` (1133-1224)

These all use `email_sender.send_messages(...)` and `email_sender.get_account_by_type` and `event.get_associated_email_account(email_sender)`. Note `send_participation_confirmation` also calls `crate::email::new_message_builder(&email_account)?` directly — that's the leak #71 needs to seal.

`send_booking_mail` (848) is for booking response mails — separate from notifications. Let me check it briefly.

Now, before #77, I need to do #71 first. Let me explore email.rs usage more.

Let me look at `models.rs` EmailAccount stuff.

parts.text:
OK so `Email::into_message` uses `crate::email::new_message_builder`. That's another leak across the seam. Per #71, this method should also be replaced. But the Email::into_message is on a model — it's called from various places (notification fns, contact, news). It calls `new_message_builder` directly.

Per #71:
- `EmailGateway` trait with 3 methods: `send_messages`, `build_message`, `account_by_type`
- "Logic modules stop calling `crate::email::new_message_builder` and `crate::email::mailbox` as free functions — those become private to the `EmailGateway` implementation."
- Delete 3 of 4 mock constructors, keep one canonical mock.
- `RealEmailGateway` (replaces `RealEmailSender`).

So the design:
- `EmailGateway` trait has 3 methods:
  - `send_messages(from: &EmailAccount, messages: Vec<Message>) -> Result<()>`
  - `build_message(account: &EmailAccount, to: &str or Mailbox, subject, ...)` — Hmm but `Email::into_message` builds via `MessageBuilder` chain. Let me think about what `build_message` should do.

Actually rethinking — `build_message(account: &EmailAccount, ...) -> Result<Message>` where `...` is "the rest" (to, subject, content / attachments). That's a wide signature.

Maybe `build_message(account)` just returns a `MessageBuilder`? Issue says "move `new_message_builder` and `mailbox` inside the module" — module being email.rs's EmailGateway impl. So `build_message` could just replace `new_message_builder`: returns a builder. But the trait method signature would be `build_message(account: &EmailAccount) -> Result<MessageBuilder>`. That essentially is still a leak (returns a builder). But the leak is sealed: logic modules call `gateway.build_message(&account)` instead of `email::new_message_builder(&account)`, and inside EmailGateway impl is the only place allowed to know about `mailbox()`.

Hmm — but the issue says trait has at most 3 methods. With `build_message` returning a builder, that's a 3-method trait: `send_messages`, `build_message`, `account_by_type`.

Wait, but the original `EmailSender` had `send_message`, `send_messages`, `test_connection`, `get_account_by_address`, `get_account_by_type`. That's 5. Issue says collapse to 3 methods. So:
- send_messages ✓
- build_message ✓
- account_by_type ✓

What about: `send_message`? It can be done via `send_messages(from, vec![msg])` — so drop it. Caller code that used `send_message` (e.g. contact.rs:39, news.rs send_mail) changes to collect into Vec and use `send_messages`. OK.

`test_connection`? Used in `tasks::check_email_connectivity`. Hmm — that's a real concern. Let me re-check the issue criteria to see if test_connection survives. Issue says: "EmailGateway trait has at most 3 methods". So test_connection must be moved out of the trait. Maybe it becomes a method on `RealEmailGateway` (not on trait) since it's only called in production tasks (not in logic). Let me check.

`get_account_by_address`? Used in `Event::get_associated_email_account`. Hmm, this is needed. Drop from trait? Then how does `get_associated_email_account` work? Well, it can use `account_by_type` for normal events; alt_email_address requires by_address lookup. Alternative: move `get_associated_email_account` out of `Event` (model) and into logic. Or: keep `get_account_by_address` and drop something else.

Wait — maybe I'm misinterpreting "at most 3 methods". Let me reread.

"Reshape the existing `EmailSender` trait in `src/email.rs` (5 methods, each a 1-line wrapper around a free function) into a deep `EmailGateway` trait with three methods:
- `send_messages(from: &EmailAccount, messages: Vec<Message>) -> Result<()>`
- `build_message(account: &EmailAccount, ...) -> Result<Message>` (move `new_message_builder` and `mailbox` inside the module)
- `account_by_type(email_type: EmailType) -> Result<EmailAccount>`"

So three specific methods listed. So `test_connection` and `get_account_by_address` and `send_message` go.

Behaviors needed:
- `test_connection` in tasks.rs — move to a `RealEmailGateway::test_connection` inherent method (only the real one needs it; tests can use a method on the struct, no longer trait).
- `get_account_by_address` — used in `Event::get_associated_email_account`. This method needs the by-address lookup. Option: change `Event::get_associated_email_account` to take `&impl EmailGateway` and only use `account_by_type`, with the `alt_email_address` resolution moved elsewhere. But that breaks the alt email behavior.

Hmm. Let me look closer at how `get_associated_email_account` is used and where alt_email_address is set.

Actually wait — maybe `account_by_type` becomes generic and the alt email lookup uses a different resolution. Or maybe `build_message` accepts the EmailAccount — but if there's an alt_email_address, we need to fetch the actual EmailAccount by address. Hmm.

Let me read the issue more carefully: "events::notifications takes &impl EmailGateway directly (the seam from #71) and stops being a sibling of the rest of events.rs for the purpose of email access."

So `events::notifications` consumes `&impl EmailGateway`. The trait has 3 methods: send_messages, build_message, account_by_type.

For `send_event_email` and `send_event_reminders` and `send_participation_confirmation` to use account_by_type or build_message — what about alt email addresses? Let me look if any test actually exercises alt_email_address path.

61: async fn send_mail(subscription: NewsSubscription, email_sender: &impl EmailSender) -> Result<()> {
62:     let primary_news_topic;
63:     let multiple_topics;
64:     if subscription.topics.len() == 1 {
65:         primary_news_topic = *subscription.topics.first().unwrap();
66:         multiple_topics = None
67:     } else {
68:         primary_news_topic = NewsTopic::General;
69:         multiple_topics = Some(
70:             subscription
71:                 .topics
72:                 .iter()
73:                 .map(|topic| topic.display_name())
74:                 .collect::<Vec<&str>>()
75:                 .join(", "),
76:         );
77:     }
78:     let subject;
79:     let topic;
80:     let kind;
81:     let regards;
82:     match primary_news_topic {
83:         NewsTopic::General => {
84:             subject = "[Infos@SVE] Bestätigung Newsletter Anmeldung";
85:             topic = "News rund um den SVE";
86:             if let Some(multiple_topics) = multiple_topics {
87:                 kind = format!(" zu folgenden Themen: {}", multiple_topics);
88:             } else {
89:                 kind = ", sobald es etwas neues gibt".into();
90:             }
91:             regards = "SV Eutingen";
92:         }
93:         NewsTopic::Events => {
94:             subject = "[Events@SVE] Bestätigung Event-News Anmeldung";
95:             topic = "unseren Events";
96:             kind = ", sobald neue Events online sind".into();
97:             regards = "Team Events@SVE";
98:         }
99:         NewsTopic::Fitness => {
100:             subject = "[Fitness@SVE] Bestätigung Fitness-News Anmeldung";
101:             topic = "unseren Fitnesskursen";
102:             kind = ", sobald neue Kurse online sind".into();
103:             regards = "Team Fitness@SVE";
104:         }
105:     };
106: 
107:     let email_account = email_sender
108:         .get_account_by_type(primary_news_topic.into())
109:         .await?;
110:     let message = crate::email::new_message_builder(&email_account)?
111:         .header(header::MIME_VERSION_1_0)
112:         .header(ContentType::TEXT_PLAIN)
113:         .to(subscription.email.parse()?)
114:         .bcc(crate::email::mailbox(&email_account)?)
115:         .subject(subject)
116:         .singlepart(SinglePart::plain(format!(
117:             "Lieber Interessent/In,
118: 
119: vielen Dank für Dein Interesse an {}.
120: 
121: Ab sofort erhältst Du automatisch eine E-Mail{}.
122: 
123: {}
124: 
125: Herzliche Grüße
126: {}",
127:             topic, kind, UNSUBSCRIBE_MESSAGE, regards
128:         )))?;
129: 
130:     email_sender.send_message(&email_account, message).await?;
131: 
132:     Ok(())
133: }

43: pub(crate) struct ResponseError {
44:     err: anyhow::Error,
45:     response: Option<(StatusCode, String)>,
46: }
47: 
48: impl Error for ResponseError {
49:     fn source(&self) -> Option<&(dyn Error + 'static)> {
50:         self.err.source()
51:     }
52: 
53:     //TODO: what to do with backtrace
54:     // fn backtrace(&self) -> Option<&std::backtrace::Backtrace> {
55:     //    self.err.backtrace()
56:     // }
57: 
58:     fn description(&self) -> &str {
59:         #[allow(deprecated)]
60:         self.err.description()
61:     }
62: 
63:     fn cause(&self) -> Option<&dyn Error> {
64:         #[allow(deprecated)]
65:         self.err.cause()
66:     }
67: }
68: 
69: impl Debug for ResponseError {
70:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
71:         write!(f, "{:?}", self.err)
72:     }
73: }
74: 
75: impl Display for ResponseError {
76:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
77:         write!(f, "{:?}", self.err)
78:     }
79: }
80: 
81: impl From<anyhow::Error> for ResponseError {
82:     fn from(err: anyhow::Error) -> ResponseError {
83:         if let Some(message) = err
84:             .downcast_ref::<ValidationError>()
85:             .map(|v| v.message.clone())
86:         {
87:             return ResponseError {
88:                 err,
89:                 response: Some((StatusCode::BAD_REQUEST, message)),
90:             };
91:         }
92:         ResponseError {
93:             err,
94:             response: None,
95:         }
96:     }
97: }
98: 
99: impl IntoResponse for ResponseError {
100:     fn into_response(self) -> Response {
101:         if self.response.is_some() {
102:             debug!("{:?}", self.err);
103:         } else {
104:             error!("{:?}", self);
105:         }
106: 
107:         self.response
108:             .unwrap_or_else(|| {
109:                 (
110:                     StatusCode::INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR,
111:                     "An internal error occurred. Please try again later.".to_string(),
112:                 )
113:             })
114:             .into_response()
115:     }
116: }

120: impl<S> FromRequestParts<S> for ClientIp
121: where
122:     S: Send + Sync,
123: {
124:     type Rejection = ResponseError;
125: 
126:     async fn from_request_parts(parts: &mut Parts, _: &S) -> Result<Self, Self::Rejection> {
127:         if let Some(ctx) = parts.extensions.get::<RequestContext>() {
128:             let source_ip = match ctx {
129:                 RequestContext::ApiGatewayV2(ctx) => ctx.http.source_ip.as_deref(),
130:                 _ => None,
131:             };
132: 
133:             if let Some(ip_str) = source_ip
134:                 && let Ok(ip) = ip_str.parse::<IpAddr>()
135:             {
136:                 debug!("Extracted client IP: {}", ip);
137:                 return Ok(ClientIp(Some(ip)));
138:             }
139:         }
140: 
141:         Ok(ClientIp(None))
142:     }
143: }

298: struct JwksCache {
299:     keys: HashMap<String, Arc<DecodingKey>>,
300:     last_updated: std::time::Instant,
301:     ttl: std::time::Duration,
302: }
303: 
304: impl JwksCache {
305:     fn new() -> Self {
306:         Self {
307:             keys: HashMap::new(),
308:             last_updated: std::time::Instant::now(),
309:             ttl: std::time::Duration::from_secs(24 * 3600), // 24 hours
310:         }
311:     }
312: 
313:     fn is_expired(&self) -> bool {
314:         self.last_updated.elapsed() > self.ttl
315:     }
316: }

318: async fn auth_middleware_fn(
319:     State(state): State<AppState>,
320:     req: Request<Body>,
321:     next: Next,
322: ) -> Response {
323:     let auth_header = match req
324:         .headers()
325:         .get("Authorization")
326:         .and_then(|h| h.to_str().ok())
327:     {
328:         Some(h) => h,
329:         None => return (StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED, "Missing Authorization header").into_response(),
330:     };
331: 
332:     let token = match auth_header.strip_prefix("Bearer ") {
333:         Some(t) => t,
334:         None => return (StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED, "Invalid Authorization header").into_response(),
335:     };
336: 
337:     let claims = match verify_session_jwt(token, &state.session_secret) {
338:         Ok(c) => c,
339:         Err(e) => {
340:             tracing::warn!("Session JWT verification failed: {:?}", e);
341:             return (StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED, "Invalid session token").into_response();
342:         }
343:     };
344: 
345:     if !authorize(&claims, &state.allowed_emails, &state.allowed_domain) {
346:         return (StatusCode::FORBIDDEN, "Access denied").into_response();
347:     }
348: 
349:     next.run(req).await
350: }
351: 
352: async fn api_key_middleware_fn(
353:     State(state): State<AppState>,
354:     req: Request<Body>,
355:     next: Next,
356: ) -> Response {
357:     let api_key = req.headers().get("x-api-key").and_then(|h| h.to_str().ok());
358: 
359:     match api_key {
360:         Some(key) if key == state.task_api_key => next.run(req).await,
361:         _ => (StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED, "Unauthorized").into_response(),
362:     }
363: }
364: 
365: async fn fetch_jwks(
366:     client: &reqwest::Client,
367:     jwks_url: &str,
368: ) -> Result<HashMap<String, Arc<DecodingKey>>> {
369:     let res = client
370:         .get(jwks_url)
371:         .send()
372:         .await?
373:         .json::<HashMap<String, Vec<Jwk>>>()
374:         .await?;
375: 
376:     let mut keys = HashMap::new();
377:     if let Some(jwks_keys) = res.get("keys") {
378:         for key in jwks_keys {
379:             if let (Some(kid), Some(n), Some(e)) = (&key.kid, &key.n, &key.e) {
380:                 let decoding_key = DecodingKey::from_rsa_components(n, e).unwrap();
381:                 keys.insert(kid.clone(), Arc::new(decoding_key));
382:             }
383:         }
384:     }
385: 
386:     Ok(keys)
387: }
388: 
389: #[derive(Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
390: struct Jwk {
391:     kid: Option<String>,
392:     n: Option<String>,
393:     e: Option<String>,
394: }
395:

397: 
398: #[derive(Deserialize)]
399: struct SessionExchangeRequest {
400:     google_token: String,
401: }
402: 
403: #[derive(Serialize)]
404: struct SessionExchangeResponse {
405:     token: String,
406: }
407: 
408: async fn exchange_session(
409:     State(state): State<AppState>,
410:     Json(req): Json<SessionExchangeRequest>,
411: ) -> Response {
412:     let claims = match verify_google_token(&req.google_token, &state).await {
413:         Ok(c) => c,
414:         Err(e) => {
415:             tracing::warn!("Google token verification failed: {:?}", e);
416:             return (StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED, "Invalid Google token").into_response();
417:         }
418:     };
419: 
420:     if !authorize(&claims, &state.allowed_emails, &state.allowed_domain) {
421:         return (StatusCode::FORBIDDEN, "Access denied").into_response();
422:     }
423: 
424:     match mint_session_jwt(&claims.email, claims.hd.as_deref(), &state.session_secret) {
425:         Ok(token) => Json(SessionExchangeResponse { token }).into_response(),
426:         Err(e) => {
427:             tracing::error!("Failed to mint session JWT: {:?}", e);
428:             (StatusCode::INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR, "Failed to issue session").into_response()
429:         }
430:     }
431: }
432: 
433: async fn verify_google_token(token: &str, state: &AppState) -> Result<Claims> {
434:     let header = decode_header(token)?;
435:     let kid = header.kid.ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing kid in JWT"))?;
436: 
437:     let decoding_key = {
438:         let needs_refresh = {
439:             let jwks_cache = state.jwks.read().await;
440:             jwks_cache.is_expired() || !jwks_cache.keys.contains_key(&kid)
441:         };
442:         if needs_refresh {
443:             tracing::info!("Refreshing JWKS cache (expired or missing kid: {})", kid);
444:             let new_keys = fetch_jwks(
445:                 &state.http_client,
446:                 "https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/certs",
447:             )
448:             .await?;
449:             let mut jwks_cache = state.jwks.write().await;
450:             jwks_cache.keys = new_keys;
451:             jwks_cache.last_updated = std::time::Instant::now();
452:         }
453:         let jwks_cache = state.jwks.read().await;
454:         jwks_cache
455:             .keys
456:             .get(&kid)
457:             .cloned()
458:             .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Unknown JWT key"))?
459:     };
460: 
461:     let mut validation = Validation::new(Algorithm::RS256);
462:     validation.validate_aud = false;
463:     let token_data = decode::<Claims>(token, &decoding_key, &validation)?;
464:     Ok(token_data.claims)
465: }
466: 
467: fn mint_session_jwt(email: &str, hd: Option<&str>, secret: &str) -> Result<String> {
468:     let now = Utc::now().timestamp() as usize;
469:     let claims = Claims {
470:         email: email.to_string(),
471:         hd: hd.map(|s| s.to_string()),
472:         exp: now + 30 * 24 * 60 * 60,
473:         iat: now,
474:     };
475:     let token = jsonwebtoken::encode(
476:         &Header::new(Algorithm::HS256),
477:         &claims,
478:         &EncodingKey::from_secret(secret.as_bytes()),
479:     )?;
480:     Ok(token)
481: }
482: 
483: fn verify_session_jwt(token: &str, secret: &str) -> Result<Claims> {
484:     let mut validation = Validation::new(Algorithm::HS256);
485:     validation.validate_aud = false;
486:     validation.validate_exp = true;
487:     let token_data = decode::<Claims>(
488:         token,
489:         &DecodingKey::from_secret(secret.as_bytes()),
490:         &validation,
491:     )?;
492:     Ok(token_data.claims)
493: }
494: 
495: fn authorize(claims: &Claims, allowed_emails: &[String], allowed_domain: &str) -> bool {
496:     allowed_emails.contains(&claims.email) || claims.hd.as_deref() == Some(allowed_domain)
497: }

501: pub(crate) fn deserialize_lifecycle_status_list<'de, D>(
502:     deserializer: D,
503: ) -> Result<Option<Vec<LifecycleStatus>>, D::Error>
504: where
505:     D: de::Deserializer<'de>,
506: {
507:     struct StringVecVisitor;
508: 
509:     impl de::Visitor<'_> for StringVecVisitor {
510:         type Value = Option<Vec<LifecycleStatus>>;
511: 
512:         fn expecting(&self, formatter: &mut fmt::Formatter) -> fmt::Result {
513:             formatter
514:                 .write_str("a string containing a comma separated list of lifecycle status strings")
515:         }
516: 
517:         fn visit_str<E>(self, v: &str) -> Result<Self::Value, E>
518:         where
519:             E: de::Error,
520:         {
521:             let mut list = Vec::new();
522:             for status in v.split(',') {
523:                 list.push(LifecycleStatus::from_str(status).map_err(E::custom)?);
524:             }
525:             Ok(match list.len() {
526:                 0 => None,
527:                 _ => Some(list),
528:             })
529:         }
530:     }
531: 
532:     deserializer.deserialize_any(StringVecVisitor)
533: }

923: /// Validates the provided captcha token using the hCaptcha service.
924: /// Returns Ok(()) if the captcha is valid, or a ResponseError if validation fails.
925: async fn validate_captcha(
926:     token: &Option<String>,
927:     client_ip: Option<IpAddr>,
928:     secrets: &dyn SecretProvider,
929: ) -> Result<(), ResponseError> {
930:     let token = token.as_ref().ok_or_else(|| ResponseError {
931:         err: anyhow::anyhow!("No captcha token provided"),
932:         response: Some((StatusCode::BAD_REQUEST, "Captcha token is required.".into())),
933:     })?;
934: 
935:     let secret = &secrets.get(SecretKey::CaptchaSecret).await?;
936: 
937:     let captcha = hcaptcha::Captcha::new(token.as_str()).map_err(|e| ResponseError {
938:         err: anyhow::anyhow!("Failed to create captcha: {:?}", e),
939:         response: Some((StatusCode::BAD_REQUEST, "Invalid captcha token.".into())),
940:     })?;
941: 
942:     let mut request = hcaptcha::Request::new(secret, captcha).map_err(|e| ResponseError {
943:         err: anyhow::anyhow!("Failed to build captcha request: {:?}", e),
944:         response: Some((
945:             StatusCode::INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR,
946:             "Captcha validation failed.".into(),
947:         )),
948:     })?;
949: 
950:     if let Some(ip) = client_ip {
951:         request = request
952:             .set_remoteip(&ip.to_string())
953:             .map_err(|e| ResponseError {
954:                 err: anyhow::anyhow!("Failed to build captcha request: {:?}", e),
955:                 response: Some((
956:                     StatusCode::INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR,
957:                     "Captcha validation failed.".into(),
958:                 )),
959:             })?;
960:     }
961: 
962:     let response = hcaptcha::Client::new()
963:         .verify(request)
964:         .await
965:         .map_err(|e| ResponseError {
966:             err: anyhow::anyhow!("Captcha verification failed: {:?}", e),
967:             response: Some((
968:                 StatusCode::BAD_REQUEST,
969:                 "Captcha verification failed.".into(),
970:             )),
971:         })?;
972: 
973:     if response.success() {
974:         Ok(())
975:     } else {
976:         Err(ResponseError {
977:             err: anyhow::anyhow!("Captcha invalid: {:?}", response.error_codes()),
978:             response: Some((StatusCode::BAD_REQUEST, "Invalid captcha token.".into())),
979:         })
980:     }
981: }

983: #[cfg(test)]
984: mod tests {
985:     use super::*;
986:     use pretty_assertions::assert_eq;
987: 
988:     fn test_secret() -> String {
989:         "test-secret-key-that-is-at-least-32-bytes!".to_string()
990:     }
991: 
992:     #[test]
993:     fn test_session_jwt_round_trip() {
994:         let secret = test_secret();
995:         let token =
996:             mint_session_jwt("admin@sv-eutingen.de", Some("sv-eutingen.de"), &secret).unwrap();
997:         let claims = verify_session_jwt(&token, &secret).unwrap();
998:         assert_eq!(claims.email, "admin@sv-eutingen.de");
999:         assert_eq!(claims.hd.as_deref(), Some("sv-eutingen.de"));
1000:         assert!(claims.exp > claims.iat);
1001:         assert_eq!(claims.exp - claims.iat, 30 * 24 * 60 * 60);
1002:     }
1003: 
1004:     #[test]
1005:     fn test_verify_rejects_expired_session_jwt() {
1006:         let secret = test_secret();
1007:         let claims = Claims {
1008:             email: "admin@sv-eutingen.de".to_string(),
1009:             hd: Some("sv-eutingen.de".to_string()),
1010:             exp: 1,
1011:             iat: 0,
1012:         };
1013:         let token = jsonwebtoken::encode(
1014:             &Header::new(Algorithm::HS256),
1015:             &claims,
1016:             &EncodingKey::from_secret(secret.as_bytes()),
1017:         )
1018:         .unwrap();
1019:         assert!(verify_session_jwt(&token, &secret).is_err());
1020:     }
1021: 
1022:     #[test]
1023:     fn test_verify_rejects_bad_signature() {
1024:         let secret = test_secret();
1025:         let wrong_secret = "a-completely-different-secret-key!".to_string();
1026:         let token =
1027:             mint_session_jwt("admin@sv-eutingen.de", Some("sv-eutingen.de"), &secret).unwrap();
1028:         assert!(verify_session_jwt(&token, &wrong_secret).is_err());
1029:     }
1030: 
1031:     #[test]
1032:     fn test_authorize_allows_allowlisted_email() {
1033:         let claims = Claims {

The new `EmailGateway` trait should have 3 methods:

- `build_message(account: &EmailAccount, ...) -> Result<Message>` — but this is tricky since build_message takes different parameters per call site (to, subject, body, attachments, bcc, reply_to...). The `...` suggests the signature is flexible. Looking at call sites:
- Most call `new_message_builder(&email_account)?` then chain `.to(...)`, `.subject(...)`, `.bcc(mailbox(&email_account)?)`, etc.
- `mailbox` is used to bcc the account's own address.

So the simplest "build_message" thatinternalizes both `new_message_builder` and `mailbox`: Actually, `new_message_builder` returns a `MessageBuilder` (not a `Message`), and the call sites chain on it. The `mailbox` is used as a `Mailbox` value for bcc.

The cleanest approach to "seal the leak" while keeping call sites working: make `EmailGateway` provide a method that returns a `MessageBuilder` (so callers can chain), OR provide a higher-level `build_message`. But the trait method returning `MessageBuilder` means the trait must expose `MessageBuilder` as a return type, which is fine since `MessageBuilder` is from lettre.

Let me write emails.rs. Key design:

```rust
// AccountLookup enum
pub(crate) enum AccountLookup<'a> {
    ByType(EmailType),
    ByAddress(&'a str),
}
```

Wait, mockall automocking a trait method with a borrowed generic arg like `&'a str` — let me make it owned `String` to be mock-friendly, or `ByAddress(String)`. Let me use owned `String`. Actually `ByAddress(&'a str)` would need lifetime param on the trait → `trait EmailGateway<'a>`, ugly. Use owned: `ByType(EmailType)`, `ByAddress(String)`. Callers pass `addr.to_string()` or `addr.clone()`. The `alt_email_address: Option<String>` is owned, so `AccountLookup::ByAddress(addr.clone())`. Fine.

But mockall: mocking `account(&self, lookup: AccountLookup) -> ...` requires `AccountLookup: Send + Sync + Clone + 'static + PartialEq + Debug` for good ergonomics with `.withf`. mockall uses them in expectations — `Eq` (PartialEq) needed for `.with(mockall::Predicate...)`. Let me derive Clone, PartialEq, Debug. And make it owned.

Now settings no explicit `#[automock]`-lifetimes needed.

`build_message` returns `Result<MessageBuilder>`. mockall requires return type to be Send + 'static? Let me check—mockall-generated mocks: for a sync method `fn foo(&self, x: X) -> T`, `T` must be `Send + 'static`? Actually mockall returns work for any T satisfies those. `MessageBuilder` likely Send + 'static. Let me proceed and handle compiler errors.

Actually `MessageBuilder::from(Mailbox)` — message builder impl. I'll proceed.

Let me write the new email.rs.
I have enough context. Creating a feature branch and implementing#71.

132:         cx: &mut std::task::Context<'_>,
133:     ) -> std::task::Poll<Result<(), Self::Error>> {
134:         self.inner.poll_ready(cx).map_err(Into::into)
135:     }
136: 
137:     fn call(&mut self, req: lambda_http::Request) -> Self::Future {
138:         let (parts, body) = req.into_parts();
139:         let body = match body {
140:             lambda_http::Body::Empty => axum::body::Body::default(),
141:             lambda_http::Body::Text(t) => t.into(),
142:             lambda_http::Body::Binary(v) => v.into(),
143:             _ => axum::body::Body::default(),
144:         };
145: 
146:         let request = axum::http::Request::from_parts(parts, body);
147: 
148:         let fut = self.inner.call(request);
149:         let fut = async move {
150:             let resp = fut.await?;
151:             let (parts, body) = resp.into_response().into_parts();
152:             let bytes = body.collect().await?.to_bytes();
153:             let bytes: &[u8] = &bytes;
154:             let resp: hyper::Response<lambda_http::Body> = match std::str::from_utf8(bytes) {
155:                 Ok(s) => hyper::Response::from_parts(parts, s.into()),
156:                 Err(_) => hyper::Response::from_parts(parts, bytes.into()),
157:             };
158:             Ok(resp)
159:         };
160:

162:     }

Actually I overcomplicated this. Let me simplify. The `mock_email_gateway` returns `(MockEmailGateway, Arc<Mutex<Vec<(EmailAccount, Vec<Message>)>>>)`, and `MockEmailGateway` already has `send_messages` configured to capture into that buffer. If I add a SECOND `expect_send_messages` returning, mockall will have two expectations — the latest one takes precedence? Actually with mockall, calling `expect_*` multiple times adds multiple expectations; each call must match one. Having two `returning` for the same method creates two "common" expectations; mockall picks the first matching. This gets messy.

Let me simplify by NOT re-configuring hear_mock for the capturing case. Instead, the test that needs just messages can flatten the batches vec.

parts.text:
No `SQLX_OFFLINE`. So the project relies on DATABASE_URL being online (`pi` host). The host `pi` is unreachable from this dev machine. The original build before my changes presumably worked — let me verify by checking if this was building before. Actually the build errors are sqlx macro expansions — they need the database at compile time OR offline cache with SQLX_OFFLINE=true.

The `.sqlx/` directory exists with cached queries. The standard sqlx setup: if SQLX_OFFLINE=true is set, macros use `.sqlx/` cache. The `cargo sqlx prepare` regenerates the cache. So to build offline here, I need `SQLX_OFFLINE=true`.

But this is a pre-existing environmental thing — the repo expects `DATABASE_URL` host `pi` (a Raspberry Pi on the local network) to be reachable. The dev machine (darwin, here) can't reach it. So the intended workflow on this machine must use `SQLX_OFFLINE=true`.

Let me check the CI build command from AGENTS.md: `cargo build --release --locked --target aarch64-unknown-linux-musl`. CI likely sets SQLX_OFFLINE=true. Let me check for a `.cargo/config.toml` or CI file.

Actually simpler: set SQLX_OFFLINE=true for the build. The `.sqlx/` cache exists, so offline should work. Let me retry.

parts.text:
All failures are `sqlx::test` macro tests needing the `pi` DB host (unreachable). The 44 passing are the non-DB tests (pure logic, contact tests, etc.). This is an environmental limitation — the `DATABASE_URL` points to a host (`pi`) reachable only on the LAN where the dev normally runs.

The `#[sqlx::test]` macro creates a fresh test database per test, requiring a live DB server. So I cannot run these integration tests here without DB access.

Let me check whether the passing tests include the ones I care about (contact, the pure ones). 44 passed. Let me see which passed — particularly contact tests and the model-level tests, and the membership `test_application_without_newsletter` (uses connect_lazy, no real DB). Let me view the summary of passing.

Actually `test_application_without_newsletter_skips_subscription` uses `PgPool::connect_lazy` and shouldn't hit DB. Did it pass? Let me find it in the output. Earlier membership.rs has `test_application_without_newsletter_skips_subscription` using connect_lazy — but it calls `news::subscribe_to_news(pool, ..., false, ...)` which calls `db::subscribe(pool, ...)` which hits the DB. So it'll fail too. Hmm.

The fundamental issue: I can't run the DB integration tests on this machine. This is an environmental constraint, not a code issue. The build compiles and clippy is clean, which means my code is type-correct. The non-DB tests pass.

I should confirm with the user whether DB tests run in their environment, OR proceed assuming the code is correct (compiles + clippy clean + non-DB tests pass) and note that DB integration tests require the `pi` host.

Actually — the AGENTS.md says `cargo sqlx prepare -- --all-targets` to update offline metadata after query changes. I didn't change queries. But maybe the build/test needs SQLX_OFFLINE AND the sqlx::test still requires a live DB. sqlx::test macros DO require a live DB even in offline mode (they spin up test DBs). So those tests are inherently environment-bound.

Let me run only the non-sqlx tests to verify my non-DB changes pass. Use `cargo test -- --skip` to skip the db-dependent ones, or run specific test modules.

Let me run the pure tests: contact tests (non-db: test_build_*, test_message_sends_to_correct_account, test_emails_groups_by_type — the last two use MockEmailGateway, no DB), api tests (non-db), models tests (test_session_jwt etc.), news tests are all sqlx::test (need DB). Let me run specific ones.

Actually `test_message_sends_to_correct_account` and `test_emails_groups_by_type_and_sends` in contact.rs are `#[tokio::test]` not `#[sqlx::test]` — pure mock. They should pass. Let me run contact tests andapi tests.
All failures are `sqlx::test` integration tests that need the `pi` DB host (unreachable on this machine). Let me verify the non-DB tests — including my new mock-based contact/email tests — pass.
